text: Static stretching performed immediately before activity reduces performance by approximately 3.7% on average.
Dynamic stretching performed immediately before activity improves performance by approximately 1.3% on average.
PNF stretching performed immediately before activity reduces performance by approximately 4.4% on average.
Static stretching of 60 seconds or more per muscle group causes greater performance deficits (-4.6%) than shorter durations under 60 seconds (-1.1%), demonstrating a dose-response relationship.
When dynamic activities are performed after static stretching as part of a warm-up, no clear negative performance effect from the stretching is observed.
All forms of stretching (static, dynamic, and PNF) improve range of motion, but the effects typically last less than 30 minutes.

A warm-up that includes stretching followed by dynamic activity is recommended for reducing muscle injuries and increasing joint range of motion with inconsequential effects on subsequent performance.
Static stretching demonstrates a moderate performance benefit of approximately 2.2% when tested at longer muscle lengths.
